behaviors.description: The keyword-frequency analyzer assigns the correct sentiment when the review contains only words from the configured positive and negative word lists.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is the local scoring logic (token counting + threshold comparison). MOCK-MISS is low if we test the real analyzer function/object directly with real config values. No OS/network/subprocess dependency (REAL-INFRA: no). Mocking is unnecessary and would not add realism here (MOCK-HIDE: no). No LLM dependence.
behaviors.test_description: Construct analyzer instances with explicit positive/negative dictionaries and thresholds, then run table-driven cases where reviews contain only configured words (including boundary counts at threshold edges). Assert exact sentiment output for each case, including case-folding/punctuation normalization cases to catch real scoring bugs.

behaviors.description: The explanation analyzer produces reasoning that references specific phrases from the original review, not generic boilerplate.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on generated language quality/grounding from a real reasoning model. A mock can easily return phrase-grounded text and hide real failures (MOCK-HIDE: yes). If analyzer is model-driven, outputs can vary run to run (LLM-DEP: yes), so minimum realistic boundary is Agentic.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real explanation analyzer/model with fixed reviews and assert the explanation includes concrete spans from the input (for example, at least one quoted or near-exact phrase match) and fails if output is template boilerplate. Run multiple trials per input to catch nondeterministic regressions in grounding behavior.

behaviors.description: When a review contains mixed signals (both positive and negative keywords), the sentiment score reflects the configured tie-breaking rule.
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic business logic in the keyword analyzer: count comparison plus tie-break policy from config. No external infra required (REAL-INFRA: no). In-process tests on the real logic catch real failures; mocks are not needed.
behaviors.test_description: Use analyzer with explicit tie-break configurations (for example, equal counts => negative, then equal counts => neutral). Feed mixed-keyword reviews with equal and near-equal counts. Assert sentiment follows configured tie rule and changes when tie configuration changes.

behaviors.description: The explanation for a sarcastic review (e.g., "Great, another broken feature") correctly identifies the negative intent despite positive surface words.
behaviors.justification: Sarcasm interpretation is semantic/model-quality behavior, not just wiring. Mocked analyzers will likely miss true failure modes (MOCK-HIDE: yes). Correctness depends on real model inference quality and can vary by run/prompt/model version (LLM-DEP: yes).
behaviors.test_description: Run the real explanation analyzer on a sarcasm-focused set (including "Great, another broken feature"). Assert output identifies negative intent and cites the sarcasm cue/context rather than literal positive wording. Execute repeated runs to detect instability in sarcasm handling.

behaviors.description: Both outputs (score and explanation) are returned together in a single response, with the explanation referencing the same sentiment direction as the score.
behaviors.justification: Returning both fields together is integration-level wiring, but the critical failure risk is real-direction mismatch caused by generated explanation content. Mocking explanation generation can make consistency always pass (MOCK-HIDE: yes). Because correctness depends on real generated text polarity alignment (LLM-DEP: yes), minimum level is Agentic.
behaviors.test_description: Exercise the real service endpoint with real sentiment scoring and real explanation generation. Assert one response contains both fields, and verify explanation polarity aligns with score (positive/negative/neutral) using explicit alignment checks over explanation text. Run repeated trials on the same inputs to catch nondeterministic contradiction failures.
